---
title: "Amra"
slug: amra
category: "Fruits, Veg & Botanicals"
category-slug: fruits-veg-botanicals
keywords: ["Hog Plum", "Indian Hog Plum", "Ambarella", "Spondias pinnata", "Pickles", "Chutney", "Sour Fruit"]
description: "Amra, botanically known as Spondias pinnata (Indian Hog Plum or Ambarella), derives its name from Sanskrit, indicating its ancient presence in India"
---

## History and Sourcing

[Amra](amra.qmd), botanically known as Spondias pinnata (Indian Hog [Plum](plum.qmd) or Ambarella), derives its name from Sanskrit, indicating its ancient presence in India. Native to tropical Asia, it thrives in eastern and southern Indian states like West Bengal, Odisha, and parts of the Northeast and South, often found wild or semi-cultivated. The fruit is typically harvested during the monsoon and post-monsoon seasons.

## Home Kitchen Use

In Indian home kitchens, [Amra](amra.qmd) is valued for its distinct tartness and fibrous pulp. Unripe fruits are widely used in tangy pickles (achar), chutneys, and refreshing summer drinks. It is a popular ingredient in regional cuisines, especially Bengali and Odia fish curries, where its sour notes complement the fish. Ripe fruits are sometimes eaten raw with salt and chili powder.

## Industrial Applications

Despite limited widespread industrial application, [Amra](amra.qmd)'s unique flavour and nutritional benefits (rich in Vitamin C and antioxidants) offer potential for modern food processing. Its pulp can be processed into concentrates for beverages, jams, jellies, and fruit bars, providing a distinctive tangy flavour. It also holds potential for use in traditional medicine formulations.

## Distinctions and Common Confusion

A common confusion arises between '[Amra](amra.qmd)' and '[Amla](amla.qmd)' (Indian Gooseberry, Phyllanthus emblica) due to similar names and tartness. However, they are distinct. Amra is larger, oval, with a tough green-to-yellow skin and a large, fibrous pit. Amla is smaller, round, smoother, with a segmented seed. Amra offers a more fibrous texture and unique aromatic complexity, making it distinct for specific culinary uses.
